TodoWrite is referenced by feature-dev even though this benchmark environment does not expose it.

The benchmark's only discriminating expectation is eval 1's calibration check ('Chooses a calibrated standard-level process...'): with_skill passes it and old_skill fails it. The other 13 expectations in this iteration pass in both configurations, so they do not currently separate the revised skill from the baseline.

Evals 0 and 2 are effectively saturated at 100% pass rate in both configurations. For this benchmark set, they confirm general competence but add little evidence about whether the revised feature-dev skill is meaningfully better than the old version.

The current data is too thin to judge flakiness because benchmark.json contains one run per eval per configuration. There is no within-eval repetition to support the variance implied by the aggregate format.

Token usage is lower with_skill on every eval in this iteration: eval 0 uses 3030 vs 3742 tokens, eval 1 uses 6932 vs 7742, and eval 2 uses 6115 vs 6696. The mean token gap is about 701 tokens in favor of with_skill.

Tool-call usage is mostly similar between configurations and does not show a consistent overhead pattern from the revised skill. with_skill uses fewer tool calls on evals 0 and 2 but slightly more on eval 1.

Error counts improve materially with the revised skill: old_skill records 6 total errors across the three evals, while with_skill records 1. The largest gap is on evals 1 and 2, where old_skill hits 3 errors in each run and with_skill hits none.

time_seconds is 0.0 for every run in both configurations, so this benchmark cannot support any real latency tradeoff analysis yet. Conclusions should be based on pass rates, tokens, tool calls, and errors rather than execution time.
